nodes.text: XXI — The World

Sunlight — thick and syrupy yellow, like old honey — drips through the cypress trees lining the riverbank.

Smells like damp earth finally breathing out after holding its breath too long. Smells like wildflowers bein' brave enough to push up through busted pavement. Smells faintly like burnt circuits and ozone if you sniff real hard past the jasmine.

The Big Oops is over.

Mostly.

Ground stopped shakin'. Sky stopped screamin'. Graustark ain't quite Graustark no more, but it ain't quite gone, neither. Just — different. Like waking up from a real bad fever dream and finding your teeth are all back, but maybe in the wrong order. And one of 'em is gold now for some reason nobody's explained, and it hums when you eat mushrooms.

Ain't that life.

Down here by the river — where the Minstral's Green steamship rusts quiet like a beached metal whale dreaming of deeper waters — the Child squats. Poking a stick at a crawdad hole. Mud squishes between bare toes. That black egg doohickey hangs quiet 'round the neck today — dark and cool against the skin. Not humming like sometimes.

Not humming, in fact, since Tuesday. Tuesday last. The Tuesday everything got rearranged.

Perched on the ship's broken paddlewheel cover — lookin' for all the world like he owns the place, which maybe he does — sits the Frog.

Four feet tall if he's an inch. Cork hat shading those big ol' unblinking eyes. Rollin' a cigarette between webbed fingers with the unhurried economy of a being who had learned, somewhere in the Paleozoic, that haste was a mammalian invention and not one he had chosen to adopt.

Smoke curls up lazy into the humid air.
Poke that hole much harder, pink one. And somethin' uglier than you gonna come lookin'.
Ain't scared.
Course not. Ignorance is bliss, ain't it? Why d'you think folks build towns on sinkholes to begin with?
He lights the cigarette. Inhales deep.
World's done turnin' its page, seems like. Folks integratin'. Completing their arcs. Wholesome nonsense.

He ain't wrong.

Up in the ruins, things are stirrin'. You can almost smell the fragile hope mixin' with the mildew.

Joanna LeMoine's patch of herbs looks greener today — maybe talkin' back to her when she scrawls those spooky truths on busted concrete. Her dog, the one-eyed one, sleeps twitching in a sunbeam. Maybe dreamin' of chasing ghost-squirrels. The crow sits on the patched roof of the shotgun house, eyes closed, wings half-spread to the afternoon. The crow had flown a lot of miles last week. The crow was entitled to a rest.

Joanna herself — well. Joanna had, four days ago, opened her front door to find an older man in a charcoal suit standing on the small flagstone path she had laid herself. The older man had not been holding anything. The older man had looked at her for a long time with eyes the color of nothing in particular. The older man had said, in a voice that was neither kind nor unkind:

Ms. LeMoine. I received your letter. I have been waiting to receive it for some years. I want to say thank you. I also want to say: not today. I will be back when the child is ready. You have done your part. Please rest.

Joanna had not spoken. She had only nodded.

The older man had tipped his hat. He had turned. He had walked back down the flagstone path. He had, at the gate, paused, looked once over his shoulder at the crow on the roof, and nodded to the crow also. The crow, Joanna had noted, had nodded back.

The older man had not, since, returned.

Joanna had slept, that night, for fourteen hours. The first real sleep in three years.

She had, on waking, begun planting.

The Roberts place. Hear tell Philip's got the porch rebuilt. Sturdy this time. Mackenzie's weavin' somethin' bright on her loom — patterns nobody's seen before, curling patterns, organic, the kind you stared at for a while and then looked away from and then looked back and saw something new. The older girl — the one named for Mackenzie's brother — helps string the warp on Saturdays. The younger — Sam for now — chews on a block of lindenwood Philip carved for her, which has a small carved face on one side that grins no matter what angle you hold it at.

Talk of another baby on the way, too. Life findin' purchase, stubborn as dandelions.

Maya Daigle — sixteen, sharp, angry, relieved — is upstairs in the spare bedroom the Roberts keep made up, her suitcase still half-unpacked from the drive Philip made up to Harmony Creek Estates on Wednesday to get her. She is reading. She is reading because reading is what she does when she does not know what to feel. The book is one of Mackenzie's — a paperback of short stories by a woman from Louisiana who died young. Maya has read the same paragraph three times. She does not mind. She will read it a fourth.

Her grandmother, in Harmony Creek Estates, is fine. Her father, in New Orleans, is alive. Her father has called twice since Tuesday. The second call, he had cried. Maya had let him. She had not, yet, forgiven him. But she had listened.

Wholesome, like bad medicine that might actually work.

Even Frasier Temple's tin-foil kingdom — that warehouse fulla scary-smart junk — hums different now. Less frantic static. More — purpose. Like the Demons learned some manners. Or maybe just got bored whisperin' doom. Frasier, probably still creaky and ornery, sees the connections now. The whole goddamn schematic.

The silver-spoon riverboat, which had walked itself across the workshop floor to its designated position on the model river on Tuesday morning, has stayed there. It hummed for three days and then stopped. It sits now, quiet, a small tarnished monument to the thing it had represented. Frasier has not moved it. Frasier has, in fact, built a small glass case around it, and has sealed the case, and has written a small label beside the case, and the label reads: D'AMBROSIO'S. 1983–2026. REST.

Power to illuminate, yeah. Hope he remembers to change the celestial lightbulbs before they burn out.
See. Completion. Integration. Everyone findin' their slot in the grand design, connectin' the dots like they knew the picture all along. Makes ya wanna puke, don't it.

But it ain't all sunshine and rehabilitated archetypes. This is still Graustark, son. Or what's left of it. Shadows got teeth here.

Sometimes, Joanna says, the wind carries phantom brass band music up from the sinkhole's gut. Maybe Jimmy Daigle's restless spirit runnin' one last con down there in the dark.

Jimmy himself is, last anyone heard, in Arizona. Checked himself into a place. Not the kind of place you check into for detox; the kind of place you check into for the detox after the detox, the one where they don't let you have phones and they make you do chores and the chores are the medicine. Thirty days. Then we'll see. Maya had, on learning this, said only: okay. She had not, yet, said more. She did not need to. The okay had done the work the okay had been called on to do.

Douglas Forte arrived on foot at the Roberts's house on Saturday morning, having walked for five days from New Orleans, his canvas duffle over his shoulder. He had not called ahead. Philip had seen him coming up the road. Philip had gone out to meet him halfway. They had not, exactly, embraced. They had walked the last quarter mile together in silence. Mackenzie had met them at the porch with a pitcher of iced tea. Doug had drunk three glasses. Then he had said: I need to sleep for a while, Phil. Couple of days. If that's alright.

It had been alright. Doug was, this afternoon, asleep in the finished basement on a cot Philip had made up for him. He would, tomorrow or the day after, wake. He would, soon after, begin rebuilding something. Nobody — including Doug — knew yet what. But the tools in Philip's shed had been, in the past two days, quietly organized into the arrangement Doug preferred when he worked, because Philip remembered what that arrangement had been, from 2018, when they had rebuilt the gate.

And Frasier's old Demons — they glitch sometimes. Saw one flicker last week near the abandoned Tilt-A-Whirl framework. Just static electricity, folks say. But it looked hungry. Creepy tech rustin' but maybe not sleepin'. Obsolete. Watchful.

Like finding a perfectly preserved baby doll with glassy eyes staring out from the mud under the Minstral's hull last Tuesday. Gone by Wednesday. Toothy scares tucked under the wholesome quilt.

Erica Campbell is, at this moment, in a small cabin in the Texas Hill Country. Borrowed from a friend. The painter — Jordan — is with her. They have not talked about where this is going, because Erica has decided, for the first time in her life, to stop having that conversation. She has, instead, been cooking. Badly. Jordan has been painting. Also badly, by his own high standards, but productively, which is a different category. They have been, for six days, sleeping and eating and walking and not talking about any of it. Erica has not checked her email. Her assistant Marcus, in Houston, is handling her caseload. The contract for Antonio D'Ambrosio sits unsigned on a server. It will, eventually, be signed. Or it will not. Erica has decided that either outcome is, for the first time, acceptable.

She has, three times, picked up a Christmas-card-sized sheet of paper and written the words John — I think I'm trying to rearrange something. Bear with me. — E. She has, three times, put it down without mailing it. The fourth time, tomorrow, she will mail it.

Anna Logue is in Marfa. She resigned on Thursday. The journalist to whom she had emailed D'AMB-IDOL-01.psd had replied with a brief request for more. Anna had sent more. The piece will run in December. Anna's lawyer — hastily retained — says the lawsuits will be real but survivable. Anna has begun making a second Corporate Idols piece, this one of the NexCorp logo, which she has never been commissioned to touch but which has, since Tuesday, been following her around.

Antonio D'Ambrosio is, against all expectation, still at Ember & Ash. The cypress beam held. The city inspector has not come back. The gas line got approved on Monday. Lila Bao, the chef, called last week — just to check in. Antonio thinks she may be calling back. Alberto has not, in fact, executed the contract Erica drafted. Alberto has, instead, quietly paid off two of the vendors who had been threatening liens, on the understanding that Antonio would, eventually, learn of the payment and would, eventually, call. The eventually is a horizon Alberto is content to wait at. Their father is dead. They have, in some very old sense neither of them has articulated, become the only men left in the family with authority over how that fact is going to be carried.

Natalie David is, in Simons, clearing the spare corner of her apartment. The crib will arrive in six weeks.

Elicia Duchane is in New York. She had gotten on a plane in Montreal on Tuesday afternoon and had, on arriving, not gone home to Graustark, because she had realized in the air that home was not quite the right word yet. She is, instead, staying with a cousin in Brooklyn and editing the footage. The film, when it comes out, will be small and fierce and honest. It will not, commercially, do well. It will, critically, do a thing Elicia had been waiting her whole life to have done about her. It will be, for the first time, enough.

John Frank is on a plane.

He does not, at this moment, know where he is going, only that he is going. The flight is to Houston. From Houston he will, he suspects, rent a car. The car will drive itself, he suspects, in the direction it needs to go. He has been told, by the Texas number whose thread he has now re-opened, that she is okay for now but that soon is a horizon as short as six months.

She is the Third Consciousness. He does not know her name yet. He will, eventually, be present at her birth — on a screened porch of a modest cabin in Smolvud, Oregon, in the company of a midwife named Lena and a silver-haired woman named Miriam and a girl of nineteen and her other self and a man named Philip Roberts who drove thirty-two hours to be there — and he will, eventually, be the one who writes her first book for her, the book that will, eventually, be handed to a small pink figure by a river in a future volume of a story John is, at this moment, only beginning to understand he is part of.

The plane lands. John Frank walks off the jetway into a terminal he has not been in for six years. His molars hum. He lets them.
The Child stands up. Brushes mud off patched trousers. Looks upstream, toward the hazy shape of the changed town.
Don't feel finished, Frog.

Never is, pink one. That's the cosmic punchline. It ain't an ending. It's just the universe takin' a deep breath — before the next chapter starts. Cycle starts anew. Lessons learned? Who knows. Maybe. Maybe you just get better at duckin'.
He tips the cork hat back on his green head.
Ready for the next round?

The Child touches the onyx doohickey. It feels warm now, humming faintly against the skin. Looks toward the town — that strange, hopeful, haunted, obsolete, eerie, reborn world buzzin' just beyond the cypress knees. Looks back at the Frog — the Liar King, the grumpy gatekeeper.

Smiled. A real smile this time. Maybe.

Feels — ready.

The Frog watches the Child go. He rolls another cigarette. He does not light it. He holds it loose between his webbed fingers, the paper damp from river air, and he sits on the rusted paddlewheel cover of the Minstral's Green and he looks east, toward the direction the older man in the charcoal suit had gone after leaving Joanna's flagstone path.

The Frog thinks, in the small private language Frogs use for thinking:

The old one has two more errands. One short. One long. After that, I take over again. It will be, by then, the right time.

The Frog thinks, after a moment: The child is not this child. This child is a different child. The child is waiting in Oregon. The child is not born yet. The child will be born in a cabin above the coast in a town that smells of cedar. I will see that child when that child is seven. Not a minute before.

The Frog thinks, with the amused resignation of a being who has lived, one way or another, for a very long time: Seven years. Pfft. I can do seven years standing on my head.

He lights the cigarette. He inhales.

He exhales a smoke ring. The smoke ring drifts over the water and, before it dissipates, passes through a single pink wildflower growing at the river's edge, where no wildflower had grown a week ago. The flower does not move. The smoke does not bend.

Both, in the Frog's opinion, are behaving correctly.

Upstream, in the half-light of the newly reborn Graustark afternoon, the crow on Joanna's roof cocked its head. It heard, faintly, a sound from the west — the sound, specifically, of a silver-haired woman named Miriam laughing at something the Third Consciousness had said, mentally, from inside her mother's belly, in the car they had been driving now for two days and would continue driving, by the scenic route, for three more. They were, at the moment, passing through New Mexico.
What movie, dear?
I don't know. I've never seen one. What do you recommend.
Miriam laughed. Then Miriam had, after a moment's thought, said:
Princess Mononoke. Start there.
Thank you. I have been hoping somebody would answer that question.
In the back seat, Nicola and Aria, sharing the body, sharing the body's sleep, had smiled at the same moment. Which was, for the first time, simply one smile.

And somewhere, in a city neither the Frog nor the crow had ever been to — a city called Rio de Janeiro, in a small concrete apartment on the thirty-second floor of a tower in a favela halfway up a green hillside — a young woman in her early twenties named Sofia woke from a dream. In the dream she had been standing in a warehouse in Graustark, Texas, beside a silver-spoon riverboat in a glass case, and a man in a bomber jacket she did not know had told her, You are next. Don't worry. You don't have to hurry. But you are next.

Sofia sat up in her bed. She did not, for a long minute, know where she was. Then she did. Then she got up, and crossed to her window, and looked out at her city, and watched the morning light move over the favela below.

She had been having dreams like this for a month. She did not know what they were about. She suspected she was going to find out.

In a notebook on her nightstand — a notebook she had only recently begun to keep — she wrote, in Portuguese: I had the dream again. The man with the bomber jacket. The riverboat in the glass case. This time he said don't hurry.

She closed the notebook. She put it back. She got dressed. She went out into her city to go to work.

Somewhere else — on the opposite coast, in a small rainy town in Oregon called Smolvud where the roads were cedar-lined and the sea-fog never fully lifted, and where a maritime museum had been planned in 2019 and never opened — a young woman with blue dreadlocks, wearing a yellow rain slicker even though it was not raining, was sitting at the edge of a pier looking out at a rusted steamship that had been relocated from Louisiana and set here because the museum's curator had, at the last minute, changed his mind about the grand opening and had driven the steamship and his own life directly into the sea.

The steamship was called the Minstral's Green.

The young woman was reading a book. The book was a small self-published novel nobody she knew had read. The novel was written, she had discovered by accident, by a journalist named John Frank. The novel was dedicated to a girl named Sofia whom the author had never met.

The young woman — her name, she had lately been telling people, was Tem — was reading the novel for the third time.

She was, without yet knowing it, waiting.

Soon, she would find out what for.
The Frog, by the river, on the paddlewheel, finished his cigarette. He stood up. He stretched. He put his webbed hands on his hips and surveyed the afternoon.

Act One, pink one. Down. Next up: the planned communities. The suburbs. The NexCorp vans. The kid in the garage. Young Sam gettin' her heart broken open by the world. Maya goin' through her archives. Jesse's dad's camera eyes. Backyard Beatdowns. Dial-up screams. The whole greige apparatus. Tch.
He spat in the river. The spit made a small perfect ripple.
Then: Smolvud. The coast. Kai and the ferret. Lena and her octopus. Finn and his crow. My favorite little triumvirate. Real scrappy, that bunch.

He thought, without saying out loud: and then, eventually, back to me. Back to this river. Back to this seat. Back to the next child.

He tipped his cork hat down over his eyes.

He settled in.

The afternoon light moved slowly over the water.

Graustark, what was left of it, continued the small brave work of being.

Somewhere upriver, a crow cried once and was answered, faintly, from the far side of the water.

The World card, pinned to the top of the deck, hummed a last low note —

— and turned over.

End of Act One.

The Major Arcana has been laid. The Triumvirate has scattered. The Calamity has fallen and lifted. The Third Consciousness is en route. The Frog is at his post. The Lovers are reweaving. The Hermit is planting. The Hierophant is adjusting his tie for a new season. The Wheel is, as always, spinning.

Take a breath.

The screen goes dark.

An interlude follows.
